lesson.menu_desc: Rich Descriptions That Bring Words to Life — past continuous for the scene — 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 15 — Describing People, Places, and Feelings: Rich Descriptions That Bring Words to Life
lesson.title_tag: Professor View — Vanessa Giuriati | Aula 15 — Rich Descriptions

listenings.text: Let me describe a morning I will never forget. It was early, and the old market was slowly waking up. The sun was rising behind the narrow streets, and a soft breeze was moving through the stalls. The square was crowded, even at that hour. People were calling out prices, and the scent of fresh bread and coffee was filling the air. An old woman was arranging gorgeous flowers in bright buckets, red, yellow, and deep purple. Two children were running between the tables while their mother was paying for fruit. Everything felt alive and peaceful at the same time. I was standing in the middle of it all, just watching, when a man started to play the violin on the corner. The whole square went quiet. For one moment, nobody was talking. We were all just listening. I still remember that gloomy old building behind him, and the warm light on the stones. It was a simple morning, but I can still see every detail.

Here is something I have learned about describing a place. Most people only say it was nice, or it was beautiful. But nice tells me nothing. I cannot see it. The secret is the small details, the ones you notice with all your senses. Do not just say the city was busy. Tell me the streets were narrow and crowded, that a cool breeze was blowing, that the scent of street food was everywhere. When you were standing there, what could you see, hear, and smell? That is what brings a description to life. And here is a useful trick for the grammar. Use the past continuous, was or were plus the ing form, to paint the background, the things that were happening around you. The sun was rising. People were talking. Then use the past simple for the one action that broke through it. Suddenly, a man started to sing. The background was moving, and one thing happened. That contrast is what makes a place feel real, like the listener was right there beside you.

extra_audio.text: First, I set the scene and say it was early, and the old market was slowly waking up. Then I describe the background, a soft breeze was blowing and the square was getting crowded. After that, I add the senses, the scent of fresh bread was filling the air and gorgeous flowers were everywhere. Suddenly, a man started to play the violin on the corner and the whole square went quiet. In the end, when I look back, I can still see every bright detail of that peaceful morning.
